era_context: The death of Tiberius and the accession of Caligula to the Roman throne. Judea under Roman procuratorial rule, with Herod Agrippa I beginning his rise to prominence. The early Christian movement in its nascent phase, centered in Jerusalem. Han Dynasty China under Guangwu, consolidating power after the collapse of the Western Han and the brief Xin Dynasty.

events.title: Caligula Becomes Roman Emperor
events.description: Gaius Julius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, known as Caligula, became Roman Emperor on September 18, 37 CE, following the death of Tiberius. Caligula was 24 years old and was initially popular with the Senate and people, as he promised to restore republican traditions and end Tiberius's reign of terror. However, his reign would quickly become characterized by erratic behavior, megalomania, and autocratic rule, lasting until his assassination in 41 CE.
events.certainty_note: The date of Caligula's accession (September 18, 37 CE) is well-established in multiple historical sources.

events.title: Herod Agrippa I Released and Appointed Tetrarch of Philip
events.description: Following Caligula's accession, Herod Agrippa I, who had been imprisoned under Tiberius, was released and appointed tetrarch of Philip (the northeastern region of Judea). This appointment marked the beginning of Agrippa's rise to prominence in Judea and reflected Caligula's initial popularity and willingness to reward supporters. Agrippa would later receive additional territories under Caligula and Claudius.
events.certainty_note: Josephus provides a detailed account of Agrippa's appointment. The date of 37 CE is well-established.

events.title: Stephen's Martyrdom (Traditional Dating)
events.description: According to the Acts of the Apostles, Stephen, a Hellenistic Jewish Christian, was tried before the Sanhedrin and executed by stoning, becoming the first Christian martyr (proto-martyr). The traditional dating of this event is around 34–35 CE, though some scholars place it as late as 37 CE. Stephen's death marked an escalation of persecution against the early Christian movement in Jerusalem and is associated with the conversion of Saul (later Paul).
events.certainty_note: The dating of Stephen's martyrdom is highly uncertain. Acts was written 30–40 years after the events it describes. Traditional dating ranges from 34–37 CE, with no contemporary sources confirming the event.

events.title: Han Dynasty Consolidation Under Guangwu
events.description: Emperor Guangwu of Han continued his consolidation of power over the fragmented territories of China. By 37 CE, the Eastern Han Dynasty had achieved relative stability and territorial control. The period was marked by the restoration of Confucian scholarship, the strengthening of the civil service system, and the reorganization of provincial administration.

sources.name: Hanshu and Later Han historiographic records

events.certainty_note: The general chronology of Guangwu's reign is well-established in Chinese historical records.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Stephen's martyrdom is highly disputed; modern scholars debate whether it occurred in 34, 35, or 37 CE, with some questioning its historicity altogether. The Acts of the Apostles is not a contemporary source and was written decades after the events it describes. Caligula's initial popularity is attested in sources, but the extent of his early reforms is debated by modern scholars.
historiographic_note: The year 37 CE is well-documented for the Roman Empire through multiple later historical sources. The accession of Caligula is one of the most securely dated events in this period. Chinese sources provide reliable documentation of Han Dynasty affairs. Early Christian history relies heavily on the Acts of the Apostles, written 30–40 years after the events, introducing significant dating uncertainty. The perspective of sources on Caligula is often biased, as he was unpopular with the Senate and later historians.

graph_edges.note: Caligula's accession led to his reign and eventual assassination
Agrippa's initial appointment led to his further territorial expansion
Stephen's martyrdom was part of early Christian persecution in Jerusalem
